name: Whisky: Scotland's Water of Life
title: The story in a glass
note: Photographs for the whisky story: the barley and the copper stills, sleeping casks, the poet-taxman, and where to try a dram in Edinburgh.

items.caption: A dram of Scotch in a Glencairn glass. The name comes from the Gaelic uisge beatha, the water of life, and it is Scotland's national drink and one of its great gifts to the world.
items.kidcaption: A glass of whisky. Its old name means 'the water of life'. It's a strong grown-ups' drink, but the story of how it's made is amazing.

items.caption: Copper pot stills, the gleaming heart of a distillery. Heat the liquid, and the alcohol boils off before the water and is caught as it rises. Their shape helps shape the flavour.
items.kidcaption: These giant copper kettles are called stills. Whisky-makers heat the liquid inside until the alcohol turns to vapour and floats up to be caught. Like a science experiment!

items.caption: A maturing warehouse. The spirit sleeps in oak casks for years, taking colour and flavour from the wood, while about two percent a year evaporates, the famous 'angels' share'.
items.kidcaption: Barrels of whisky sleeping in the dark for years and years. A little bit floats away into the air each year, and the Scots say the angels drink it. The angels' share!

items.caption: It all starts here: a field of barley. Barley, water, yeast and time, plus oak and sometimes peat smoke, are very nearly all that goes into a single malt.
items.kidcaption: Whisky starts as a field of barley, a kind of grain. Add water, a drop of yeast, some smoke and a lot of patience, and that's almost the whole recipe.

items.caption: Irish stills. Irish whiskey is often distilled three times for a smoother spirit and is rarely smoky, one of the clearest differences from Scotch, which is usually distilled twice.
items.kidcaption: Whisky is made all over the world. Irish whiskey is usually cooked three times instead of two, which makes it smoother. Different countries, different recipes.

items.caption: Robert Burns, Scotland's national poet, spent his last years as an exciseman, a taxman who hunted the illegal stills hidden in the glens. Whisky's early history is a smugglers' tale.
items.kidcaption: For years whisky was made in secret to dodge the taxman. And guess who worked as a whisky taxman? The famous poet Robert Burns himself!

items.caption: Curving Victoria Street in the Old Town, home of the Bow Bar, one of Edinburgh's classic whisky pubs, with hundreds of bottles on a gantry rescued from an old church.
items.kidcaption: Pretty, curvy Victoria Street. Tucked along here is the Bow Bar, a famous old pub where grown-ups can choose from hundreds of different whiskies.

items.caption: The Scotch Whisky Experience on Castlehill, by the castle: a family-friendly barrel ride through how whisky is made, and home to the world's largest whisky collection.
items.kidcaption: The Scotch Whisky Experience, right by the castle. You ride through in a barrel to see how whisky is made, and there's the biggest whisky collection on Earth to gawp at!
